coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe of quantum dot-embedded imprinted polymer used with a portable smartphone-assisted color analysis to determine diuron
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pongprom, Angkana
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent probe was successfully developed using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) decorated porous zinc oxide (ZnO) embedded in a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP sensing probe was synthesized via the sol-gel polymerization method and exhibited a strong fluorescence emission at 403 nm. Applied to detect trace levels of diuron determined by spectrophotometry, the fluorescent sensor exhibited a good linear response within the range of 0.10–50.0 μg L−1 with a coefficient of determination of 0.9997. The limit of detection was 0.07 μg L−1. Coupled with a smartphone-assisted fluorescence color analysis platform, the probe produced linearity in the range of 5.0–50.0 μg L−1, with an R2 of 0.9986 and a limit of detection of 2.5 μg L−1. Both developed methods successfully determined diuron in radish, tomato, cucumber and green tea. Recoveries were between 90.5 and 107.3 % with RSDs <8.0 % for the fluorescent sensor coupled with spectrophotometry and 91.5–105.3 % with an RSD of <7 % for the smartphone-assisted method. The reliability and accuracy of both methods were confirmed by comparison with high-performance liquid chromatography. The proposed fluorescent probe coupled with the smartphone-assisted color analysis platform provided high sensitivity and good selectivity while being simple, convenient, rapid and inexpensive. The method has several application possibilities for on-site detection of trace diuron.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Smartphone-based sensing platform
Quantum dots
Diuron
Porous zinc oxide
Molecularly imprinted polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the nanocomposite fluorescent probe
On-site determination of diuron using smartphone-assisted platform
Quantum yield of N-GQDs and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe
Characterization of fluorescence probe and related materials
Optimization of the fabrication of nanoprobe and determination condition
Effect of template to monomer to cross-linker ratio
Effect of pH of the dispersion solution
Quantitative detection performance and quenching mechanism
Stability and reproducibility of fluorescence probe
Analysis of diuron in radish, tomato, cucumber and green tea samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science, Research and Innovation Fund

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6801170S). Angkana Pongprom was supported by the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program (RGJ-PHD) (N41A661170), National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT). Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne is also acknowledged for English proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe for the determination of diuron.

The components of the smartphone-assisted platform for on-site determination of diuron consisted of a black box (A), a 400 nm light filter (B), a smartphone (C) and a 365 nm UV-lamp supplied by a portable power bank (D).

TEM image of N-GQDs (A). The inset histogram shows the particle size distribution range. A photograph of N-GQDs under UV light at 365 nm (B). The SEM image (C) and XRD patterns (D) are of porous ZnO NPs. The SEM image of the synthesized ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe (E). FT-IR spectra (F) of N-GQDs (curve a), porous ZnO NPs (curve b) and the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe (curve c).

The effect of incubation time on the change of fluorescence quenching (F0/F) of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe in a diuron concentration of 1.0 μg L−1 (A). The charts show the effects of parameter variation on the sensitivity of the response of the probe. The varied parameters were the amount of ZnO NPs used to fabricate the probe (B), the mole ratio of template to monomer and cross-linker in the MIP (C), the MIP polymerization time (D), the removal solvent type (E) and the pH of the phosphate buffer probe dispersion (F). The sensitivity of the probe response was obtained from the slope of the calibration curve in the diuron concentration range of 0.1–50.0 μg L−1.

Fluorescence emission spectra are of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe (green lines) in the presence of diuron from 0.10 to 50 μg L−1 (the inset shows corresponding photographs) (A). The same responses are shown for the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP probe (B). The linear relationships between F0/F and diuron concentration are shown for the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP (green line) and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP probes (red line) (C). The chart shows the sensitivity of non-molecularly imprinted and molecularly imprinted polymer probes with and without ZnO NPs (D). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The results of smartphone-assisted analysis of fluorescence emissions at different concentrations of diuron in the developed fluorescent probe under 365 nm UV light (A). The linear relationship between I mean and diuron concentrations from 5.0 to 50.0 μg L−1 was obtained with the smartphone-assisted analysis platform (B). The linear relationship between F0/F and diuron concentration was compared when obtained from spectrophotometric analysis and smartphone-assisted analysis of probe fluorescence quenching by diuron concentrations from 5.0 to 50.0 μg L−1 (C).
The chart shows the selectivity of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe toward diuron (A). The competitive binding between ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP and diuron in the presence of monuron at different concentrations (B).
Determination of diuron in food samples using the developed fluorescent sensor, HPLC and smartphone-assisted color analysis.
Analytical performances of analytical methods reported for the determination of diuron.

row.entry:
Paddy field water,
paddy soil,
grain seedlings

Well water, lake water,
irrigation ditch water

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe of quantum dot-embedded imprinted polymer used with a portable smartphone-assisted color analysis to determine diuron

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent probe was successfully developed using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) decorated porous zinc oxide (ZnO) embedded in a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP sensing probe was synthesized via the sol-gel polymerization method and exhibited a strong fluorescence emission at 403 nm. Applied to detect trace levels of diuron determined by spectrophotometry, the fluorescent sensor exhibited a good linear response within the range of 0.10–50.0 μg L−1 with a coefficient of determination of 0.9997. The limit of detection was 0.07 μg L−1. Coupled with a smartphone-assisted fluorescence color analysis platform, the probe produced linearity in the range of 5.0–50.0 μg L−1, with an R2 of 0.9986 and a limit of detection of 2.5 μg L−1. Both developed methods successfully determined diuron in radish, tomato, cucumber and green tea. Recoveries were between 90.5 and 107.3 % with RSDs <8.0 % for the fluorescent sensor coupled with spectrophotometry and 91.5–105.3 % with an RSD of <7 % for the smartphone-assisted method. The reliability and accuracy of both methods were confirmed by comparison with high-performance liquid chromatography. The proposed fluorescent probe coupled with the smartphone-assisted color analysis platform provided high sensitivity and good selectivity while being simple, convenient, rapid and inexpensive. The method has several application possibilities for on-site detection of trace diuron.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diuron is a phenylurea herbicide used in agriculture and horticulture [1]. It is effective against weeds in crops such as sugarcane, cotton, citrus and soybean [2]. Diuron is also utilized in non-agricultural areas like roadsides and industrial sites. However, diuron residues can persist in soil for a long time and pose risks to non-target plants and the environment [3]. It can also leave residual traces in food that can be toxic and harmful to human health, causing vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dizziness, headache, weakness and confusion [4,5]. Several regulatory agencies, including the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the European Union (EU) have established maximum residual limits for diuron in vegetables that range from 0.01 to 0.5 mg kg−1 [6,7]. Therefore, the precise measurement of diuron is necessary to protect public health.

The various analytical techniques used to determine diuron include electrochemical sensing [8,9], liquid chromatography mass spectrometry [10,11], gas chromatography [12], fluorescence spectroscopy [13] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14]. HPLC is a powerful, sensitive, versatile and precise analytical technique [15], but is expensive and requires skilled personnel and large amounts of solvent [16]. Alternative approaches based on fluorescence quenching have gained considerable attention. Fluorescence-based methods are also highly sensitive but are easy to use and rapid [17,18].

Fluoresence-based sensors require a fluorescent probe. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials that offer photostability, low toxicity, biocompatibility and easy functionalization [19,20]. Nitrogen atom doping of GQDs can change their electrical characteristics and add more active sites, new features, and enhanced attributes [21]. Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) are an attractive fluorescent probe because the nitrogen atoms enhance their photoluminescence, fluorescence quantum yield (QY), bandgap tunability and biocompatibility [22]. To improve the selectivity of the response of N-GQDs to a target molecule, they can be integrated with high affinity materials such as molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). MIP is an interesting material produced by designing and synthesizing specific binding sites or imprints within a polymer matrix that are compatible with the chemical functionality, size, and shape of a target molecule [23,24]. MIP can be synthesized by sol-gel [25], precipitation [26], bulk [27] and suspension polymerization [28]. Sol-gel polymerization is a synthesis method that enables good control of composition, porosity and morphology. A second high-affinity material integrated into the fluorescent probe can reduce the time required for the necessary binding to occur between the embedded probe and the target analyte. To enhance the affinity between the fluorescent probe and target analyte, zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) can be incorporated in the probe to improve adsorption ability, resulting in increased sensitivity [29].

In this work, a fluorescent probe was developed by incorporating ZnO and N-GQDs within an MIP (ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP). The performances of the fluorescent sensor were thoroughly investigated. The sensor was used to detect diuron in real samples and the results were compared with the results obtained with HPLC. Additionally, this newly developed fluorescent probe can be integrated in a smartphone-assisted color analysis platform to replace a laboratory spectrophotometer.

Diuron and citric acid were obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd., Japan. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 100 %w/v) was sourced from Sigma-Aldrich, Italy; 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 99.0 %w/v) was from Sigma-Aldrich, USA; tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (THAM) was from Sigma-Aldrich, Spain; and zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn (Ac)2.2H2O) was from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. Sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate, sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate and tri-sodium citrate were obtained from Ajax Finechem, Australia. Urea (CO(NH2)2) was obtained from KEMAUS, Australia. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets, methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH) and acetonitrile (ACN) were supplied by RCI Labscan, Thailand. Ammonia solution (25.0 % w/v) was obtained from Merck, Germany.

Fluorescence emissions were measured using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Light absorbption spectra were acquired using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600i, Shimadzu, Japan). The data from a surface area and porosity analyzer (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA) was used to calculate the Branauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of samples. The morphology of fluorescent probes and associated materials were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEM-5200, JEOL, Japan). Functional groups of the materials were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometry (Spectrum GX FTIR, PerkinElmer, USA).

ZnO NPs were synthesized following a previous method [30] in which 0.70 g of zinc acetate, 0.40 g of urea and 80.0 mg of tri-sodium citrate were dissolved in 75.0 mL of DI water and stirred for 30 min. The obtained mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 120 οC for 6 h. The mixture was separated via centrifugation and the pellet washed with ethanol and DI water to remove impurities. Finally, the pellet was dried at 80 οC to obtain a ZnO NPs.

N-GQDs were prepared following a previous report [31]. Initially, 2.00 g of citric acid and 0.50 g of tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane were heated to 220 °C until the mixture attained a pale-yellow color. The mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature (27 ± 1 °C), giving a brown solid product of N-GQDs. The synthesized N-GQDs were then pH-adjusted to neutral using 1.0 M NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nanocomposite fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe was fabricated in a sol-gel polymerization process. Initially, 5.80 mg of diuron, 5.0 mL of methanol and 90.0 μL of APTES were mixed and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, 5.0 mL of the synthesized N-GQDs were introduced into the mixture and stirred for 1 h. Then, 1.00 mg of ZnO, 0.20 mL of TEOS, and 150 μL of NH3 were added and the stirring continued for 4 h. The template was removed from the resultant nanocomposite probe by washing it with 10.0 mL of methanol. Subsequently, the probe was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The template was completely removed from the probe by repeating the washing procedure three times. The prepared ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe was subsequently dried at 60 °C. A non-imprinted polymer nanocomposite probe (ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP) was also synthesized without the addition of the diuron template. The fabrication of the nanoprobe is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Fluorescence emission measurements were carried out at room temperature using the following procedure. Initially, 1.00 mg of ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe was dispersed in 15.0 mL of 10 mM phosphate buffer solution (pH = 10.0). Then, 100 μL of diuron standard (from 0.10 to 50.0 μg L−1) or sample solution were combined with 300 μL of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP suspension and incubated for 16 min. The emission spectra of the probe suspension in the presence or absence of diuron were recorded after setting the excitation at 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site determination of diuron using smartphone-assisted platform
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the components of the smartphone-assisted fluorescence measurement system. Inside a black box, a 365 nm UV-lamp covered with a 400 nm light filter was supplied by a portable power bank. A smartphone was also connected to the power bank and the camera lens was positioned in line with the sample cell. To perform the smartphone-assisted fluorescence measurement, 4.0 mL of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe were mixed with 1.0 mL of diuron standard or sample solution. Then, 2.0 mL of the mixture were placed in a centrifuge tube and incubated for 16 min. The incubated sample was transferred to the sample cell and placed in the dark box. The distance between the sample cell and the UV lamp was 6 cm. The sample was illuminated and the color information of captured images was converted into R, G and B values by the Color Picker application loaded on the smartphone.

Radish, tomato and cucumber samples were obatined from a local supermarket and pretreated following a reported method [32]. Briefly, 50.0 g of homogenized sample were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask. The obtained precipitation was mixed with 5.0 mL of acetonitrile to extract the target analyte, vortexed for 5 min, and then centrifuged for 10 min. The upper, liquid phase was collected, and its volume was adjusted to 100 mL with deionized water. The sample solution was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter before analysis. Green tea beverage samples were acquired from local retail shops. Each 1.00 mL of green tea was diluted 10-fold with deionized water and filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter prior to analysis.

The concentration of diuron was quantitatively analyzed using an 1100 series HPLC system (Agilent Technologies, Germany). Chromatographic separations were performed in a Fortis C18 column (4.6 mm × 15 cm, 5 μm). The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and water (55:45, v/v) flowed at 1.00 mL min−1. The injection volume was 20 μL. The detection wavelength was set at 244 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield of N-GQDs and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The QYs of the synthesized N-GQDs and the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe were determined at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm, using quinine sulfate in 0.10 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54) as a standard reference. QY was calculated from the following equation [33]:
QY =
QY R
(
I S
A R
(
η S
)
2
)
/
(
I R
A S
(
η R
)
2
)
where the subscripts R and S indicate the reference and the prepared sample, respectively. I is the integrated fluorescence emission intensity and A is the absorbance intensity. η is the solvent refractive index (η = 1.33). Various concentrations of N-GQDs, ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe, and quinine sulfate were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fluorescence probe and related materials
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of N-GQDs was observed by TEM. The TEM images (Fig. 3 A) revealed the uniform distribution and relatively consistent size of the synthesized N-GQDs. The particle size ranged from 1.5 to 5.0 nm, with an average size of 3.0 nm. The N-GQDs were then illuminated under UV light. Upon UV excitation, electrons in the N-GQDs transitioned to higher energy states and when the electrons returned to the ground state, the N-GQDs emitted a bright-blue fluorescence (Fig. 3B), indicating the successful synthesis of the N-GQDs. SEM images of porous ZnO NPs (Fig. 3C) showed spherical particles with rough, coral-like surfaces. The structure of the porous ZnO NPs was determined by XRD analysis (Fig. 3D). The Bragg reflections observed at approximately 31.9°, 34.5°, 36.4°, 47.8°, 56.6°, 63.0°, and 68.2° 2θ corresponded to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), and (112) planes of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO. The SEM image of ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP (Fig. 3E) shows uniform spherical particles on the surfaces of the porous ZnO NPs, which confirmed the success of the polymerization procedure and the smooth polymer coating.

FT-IR spectra were utilized to evaluate the chemical functional groups of N-GQDs, ZnO NPs, and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP (Fig. 3F). In the spectrum of N-GQDs, the absorption band at 3411 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–H and N–H groups. The peaks at 1578 and 1396 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching and N–H bending, respectively. Additionally, the peak at 1218 cm−1 was identified as C–O stretching, while the band around 786 cm−1 indicated N–H wag vibration. These results confirmed the successful integration of nitrogen atoms into the GQDs. In the spectrum of porous ZnO NPs, the absorption peaks observed at 3407 and 1578 cm−1 were attributed to the O–H stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1400 cm−1 indicated the presence of CO stretching from the precursor decomposition. The weak peak at 1045 cm−1 was assigned to C–O stretching, while the peak at 832 cm−1 was associated with the flexing vibration of porous ZnO. This evidence denoted the successful hydrothermal synthesis of ZnO NPs. In the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP spectrum, the broad peak at 3423 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching of –OH and –NH from hydroxyl and amino groups. The peak at 1571 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching of CO groups of N-GQDs. The peaks at 1055, 786, and 583 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching, and bending vibrations of the Si–O–Si (siloxane) group, respectively. The peak at 695 cm−1 was assigned to the Zn–O stretching of porous ZnO NPs. The above observations confirmed the successful formation of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP fluorescent probe.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to confirm the surface functional groups of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe, and the results are shown in Fig. S1. The survey spectrum of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe was shown in Fig. S1A, which exhibited six distinct peaks at binding energies of 104.0 (Si 2p), 285.0 (C 1s), 400.0 (N 1s), 533.0 (O 1s), 1022.0, and 1045.0 (Zn 2p) eV. The atomic percentages of silicon, carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and zinc were 21.60, 24.60, 11.08, 31.12, and 12.01 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of Si 2p (Fig. S1B) showed peaks at 102.1 eV that were attributed to Si–O vibration. The spectrum of C 1s in Fig. S1C presented a peak at 284.8, 285.8, 286.8, 288.5, and 289.4 eV, corresponding to CC, C–N, C–O, CO, and O–CO, respectively. The spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S1D) exhibited peaks at 399.5, 400.7, and 402.1 eV related to graphitic nitrogen structures, amino groups and quaternary-N. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. S1E), four peaks were observed at 2531.0, 532.4, 532.9, and 534.3 eV, which are assigned to the following functional groups, including –OH, Si–O–Si, O–CO, and H2O. The spectrum of Zn 2p (Fig. S1F) presented a two-peak at 1021.9 and 1045.0 eV, which are attributed to Zn–OH and Zn–N–C, respectively. These results confirmed that the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP nanoprobe was successfully synthesized and ensured the presence of ZnO and N-GQDs in the MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the fabrication of nanoprobe and determination condition
ce:section.ce:para: The following parameters were optimized to improve the determination of diuron with the fluorescent nanoprobe. The parameters included probe–analyte incubation time, ZnO NPs loading, mole ratio of template to monomer and cross-linker in the MIP, MIP polymerization time, removal solvent type and phosphate buffer dispersion solution pH. Three replicates of each experiment were performed. The optimization of binding time was evaluated based on fluorescence quenching (F0/F), while the other parameters were optimized based on a sensitivity analysis. The sensitivity of the nanoprobe was calculated from the slope of a linear equation.

The incubation time is an important factor in the detection procedure. Enough time must be allowed for the target molecule to quench probe fluorescence. The change in F0/F was evaluated within a range of 2–30 min. The F0/F of ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP reached a peak at 16 min, then remained constant (Fig. 4 A). Therefore, the optimal incubation time was 16 min.

The amount of ZnO NPs embedded into the nanocomposite probe was studied within the range of 0.50–5.00 mg. As shown in Fig. 4B, the highest sensitivity was observed at 1.00 mg. Excessive ZnO can interfere with the proper formation of specific binding sites in the MIP, leading to reduced sensitivity. On the other hand, a smaller amount of ZnO also results in low sensitivity due to the low number of specific binding sites formed. Therefore, the nanoprobe was prepared using 1.0 mg of ZnO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of template to monomer to cross-linker ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of specific binding sites depends on the mole ratio of the polymerization precursors. The effect of the template to monomer (TE:MO) ratio was investigated at ratios of 1:10, 1:15 and 1:20, and the effect of the template to crosslinker (TE:CR) ratio at 1:30, 1:40 and 1:50 (Fig. 4C). At a TE:MO ratio of 1:20, excessive MO (APTES) hindered the formation of specific recognition sites in ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP and caused non-specific interactions between the MO and the TE (diuron). However, a negligible amount of MO provided insufficient –NH2 groups to interact with diuron, resulting in the low sensitivity at a TE:MO ratio of 1:10. The large amount of CR at the 1:50 ratio led to a tightly packed polymer network which obscured or blocked binding sites. The sensitivity was also low when the TE:CR ratio was 1:30. The smaller amount of CR caused the distribution of recognition cavities inside the nanoprobe to be less uniform with low stability. Therefore, the ratio of TE:MO:CR of 1:15:40 was used to fabricate the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP nanoprobe.

The effect of polymerization time was evaluated to ensure the complete formationof specific binding sites within the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe. The polymerization time was studied from 3.0 to 5.0 h. The best sensitivity was obtained when the polymerization time was 4 h (Fig. 4D). The lower sensitivity of the probes polymerized for 3.0 and 3.5 h was due to the incomplete formation of binding sites. However, polymerization times longer than 4.5 and 5.0 h produced probes that exhibited low sensitivity due to over-crosslinking, which generated rigid polymers and hindered interaction between the probes and diuron. A polymerization time of 4 h was selected for the synthesis of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe.

The effect of the removal solvent was evaluated to ensure the complete removal of diuron from the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe. The studied solvents were water, acetonitrile, methanol and ethanol. The most efficient solvent that produced the probe with the highest sensitivity was methanol (Fig. 4E). Since methanol and diuron possess similar polarities, this solvent could most easily break the bonds between diuron and the probe.
Consequently, methanol was selected as the removal solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of the dispersion solution
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of dispersion solution pH was studied in the range of 9.0–13.0. As shown in Fig. 4F, the highest sensitivity was obtained at pH 10.0. At pH values above 10.0, sensitivity decreased. Although the alkaline condition did not affect the structure of diuron (pKa value of 13.5) [34], the silica shell of the polymer could be degraded. Additionally, the functional groups on the surface of the N-GQDs could be ionized, leading to surface defects. Furthermore, when pH was 9.0, sensitivity was also low, which was attributed to the increased presence of hydrogen ions in the solution that could damage the MIP. Therefore, pH 10.0 was selected as the most suitable dispersion solution pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection performance and quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of diuron with the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP probes was investigated in the optimal condition using a diuron concentration range of 0.10–50 μg L−1. The intensity of the fluorescence emissions of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe significantly decreased with increasing diuron concentration (Fig. 5 A). The responses can be seen in the photographs of the probe under UV light at 365 nm. These results indicated that the specific recognition sites of ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP were highly selective for diuron. In contrast, the emissions of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP toward diuron showed negligible change due to the absence of specific binding sites between diuron and the NIP probe (Fig. 5B). The analyte was still detected due to non-specific binding between the analyte and the surface of the NIP probe. The linear diuron detection range was determined to be 0.10–50.0 μg L−1, with regression equations of F0/F = 0.0771Cdiuron + 1.5233 for the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe (R2 = 0.9997), and F0/F = 0.0277Cdiuron + 1.5214 for the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP probe (R2 = 0.9954) (Fig. 5C). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.07 μg L−1, calculated from 3.3σ/slope (n = 20), where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal.

To assess the specificity of the imprinted cavities formed on the nanoprobe's surface, specificity is defined by the imprinting factor (IF). The IF was found to be 2.8 by comparing the slope of the calibration curve for the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe to that of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP probe that wasn't imprinted. The findings indicated that the imprinted cavities of the synthesized ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe exhibited high specificity for the detection of diuron.

The performances of the N-GQDs∗NIP, ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP, N-GQDs∗MIP and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP were compared. The sensitivity of the four sensing probes was obtained from the slope of the calibration curves for the determination of diuron in the concentration range of 0.1–50.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 5D and Fig. S2). The N-GQDs∗NIP and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP demonstrated low sensitivity due to the low number of specific binding sites on the probes. The ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP demonstrated greater sensitivity than the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP because it offered a greater affinity for diuron, resulting in stronger and more specific interactions. The emission intensity of the N-GQDs∗MIP probe decreased with increasing diuron concentration; however, the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe exhibited more remarkable fluorescence quenching than N-GQDs∗MIP (Figs. S3A and S3B). The results demonstrated that the incorporation of ZnO into the composite sensing probe can improve the sensitivity for the determination of diuron.

The smartphone-assisted fluorescence color analysis platform for onsite determination comprised a smartphone, a sample cell, a switch, a portable power bank, a 365 nm UV lamp, and a dark box measuring 220 mm (width) × 270 mm (length) × 145 mm (height). Applying the Color Picker application on the smartphone, the recorded fluorescent color signal was converted to RGB values (Fig. 6 A). For the quantitative analysis of diuron, RGB values were used to calculate grayscale values (I) from the following equation [35]: I = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B. The plot between the mean grayscale values (I mean = (I blank - I test)/I blank) and diuron concentration was constructed. A linear correlation was observed in the range of5.0–50.0 μg L−1 (Fig. 6B), represented by the following regression equation:I mean = 0.0122Cdiuron + 0.3696 (R2 = 0.9986). The LOD was 2.5 μg L−1. Moreover, the slopes of the linear relationships between F0/F and diuron concentration produced by the fluorescent probe coupled with the spectrophotometer and with the smartphone-assisted platform showed consistency (Fig. 6C). These results confirmed that the smartphone-assisted fluorescence color analysis platform could be effective for the on-site determination of diuron.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism between ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP anddiuron is illustrated in Fig. S4. The absorption spectrum of diuron (brown dotted line at255 nm) did not overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe (red line peak at 403 nm). As a result, fluorescence quenching was not caused by energy transfer but was due to electron transfer. Additionally, the fluorescence quenching was explained by the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Cdiuron] [36]where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of diuron, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Cdiuron] indicates the concentration of diuron.

The fluorescence quenching effects of diuron and the structural analogs monuron, chlortoluron, isoproturon, and buturon were compared to evaluate the selectivity of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP and ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP probes. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe was more selective than the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP due to the high-affinity binding between diuron and the recognition sites of the probe that the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗NIP lacked. In addition, the sensitivity of ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP towards chemical structural analogs of diuron was significantly lower because there were no specific recognition sites for the analogs. The results indicated that the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP exhibited superior selectivity towards diuron. To confirm that the analogs had no effect on the detection of diuron, the competitive binding between diuron and monuron was studied. The intensity of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe remained unchanged when the concentration of monuron was increased (Fig. 7B), indicating that the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe was highly selective for diuron.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe was investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe every 30 min. The fluorescence intensity of theZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe showed no significant change over 300 min (Fig. S5A). The reproducibility of the ZnO∗N-GQDs∗MIP probe (Fig. S5B) was investigated by preparing six fabrication lots at different times to determine diuron in the optimal condition. The RSD of the responses of the probe was 1.2 %, indicating the good reproducibility of the nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of diuron in radish, tomato, cucumber and green tea samples
ce:section.ce:para: The matrix effect (ME) for the determination of diuron using the fluorescent sensor and smartphone-assisted fluorescence color analysis platform was evaluated by comparing the calibration curves of diuron in spiked food samples and spiked methanol (Table S1). The ME of the fluorescent sensor and smartphone-assisted fluorescence color analysis platform was calculated according to the following equation, ME (%) = ((slope of calibration curve of spiked sample/slope of calibration curve of spiked solvent)-1) × 100. The MEs of fluorescent sensor and smartphone-assisted analysis ranging from −10.2 to 8.0 % and −3.3 to 1.6 %, respectively. The results demonstrated that the matrix effect have minimal or no effect on the quantitative analysis in real samples.

A fluorescent probe was successfully developed using N-GQDs and porous ZnO incorporated into an imprinted polymer. A smartphone-assisted color analysis platform was also developed to measure the quenching of probe fluorescence by the phenylurea herbicide diuron in food samples. The developed method exhibited selectivity, sensitivity and reliability.
The fluorescent probe was coupled with a spectrophotometer and the smartphone-assisted platform, demonstrating respective recovery ranges of 90.5–107.3 % and 91.5–105.3 %.

The analysis results also agreed well with HPLC analysis. The fluorescent probe is easy to fabricate, and when coupled with both fluorescence measurement platforms produced cost-effective results rapidly. The smartphone-assisted color analysis platform has significant potential for on-site detection due to its performance and portability. The proposed strategy can be adapted to determine other analytes in various sample complexes.

Angkana Pongprom: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Opas Bunkoed: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6801170S). Angkana Pongprom was supported by the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program (RGJ-PHD) (N41A661170), National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT). Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne is also acknowledged for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:para:
